The browser is connected now. I’m navigating to the target page, extracting structure and behavior first, then I’ll decide the minimum scaffold needed in this repo to reproduce it.I’ve got the target page loaded. Next I’m pulling the live text, screenshots, and DOM/style data so the clone is based on the actual page rather than guesswork.I have enough of the live page to start: it’s a single-route marketing page with Lenis-style smooth scrolling, a large hero, install cards, a feature strip, and a pricing/footer block. I’m now checking the October project metadata so I can scaffold the clone in the right shape instead of inventing the wrong app structure.I’ve confirmed this is a blank folder, so the clone requires bootstrapping a web app here rather than modifying an existing one. Before I install anything, I’m doing one more extraction pass for typography, colors, assets, and the lower-page layouts so the implementation has the right foundation.I’m switching from extraction to implementation. Because this folder has no app scaffold, I’m going to create a minimal Vite + React app here, reproduce the Hermes landing page as a single-screen clone, and keep the visual assets remote first so the page can build cleanly before any optional asset-localization pass.The app scaffold and initial clone implementation are in place. I’m installing the declared dependencies now so I can run the build and catch any issues rather than leaving this as unverified source only.While the install runs, I’m checking the current source for likely first-pass issues so I can fix them immediately after the build instead of waiting for a failure round-trip.Dependencies are installed. I’m running the build now and will fix any compile or bundling issues before I hand this back.Built a new Vite + React app in this folder and implemented a single-screen Hermes landing page clone with the same main sections, copy, blue/white visual treatment, hero art, install cards, feature showcase, and pricing/footer block. I added all required dependencies to package.json and verified it builds successfully with npm run build.

This is a first-pass clone, not a pixel-perfect reconstruction yet. It currently uses remote Hermes asset URLs and approximated typography/behavior rather than downloaded local assets and exact scroll effects.

From `/Users/netha/Documents/project/new`, run `npm run dev`, then open the local Vite URL it prints, typically `http://localhost:5173/`.

If you want, I can start the dev server for you in this workspace.

**Summarizing improved clone features**

The first pass was only a rebuild, not a true mirror. I’m switching to a stricter approach now: extract the real shipped HTML/CSS/JS structure from the live page and replace the approximation with a static mirror so the animations and interaction model come across intact.I’ve confirmed the original page ships most of its fidelity through real class-heavy markup plus the site’s own stylesheet, with only a smaller amount of JS behavior layered on top. I’m extracting the exact `hermes-web` HTML and stylesheet in chunks now so I can replace the approximation with a much closer mirror.The fastest path to a near-exact clone is to reuse the site’s shipped stylesheet and mirror its actual DOM structure, then fill the few live behaviors with local JS. I’m pulling the remaining HTML chunk and the interaction hooks now so I can swap out the handmade React layout.I have the real DOM structure now, including the feature parallax and fixed footer mechanics. I’m replacing the custom CSS implementation with a DOM/class mirror that uses the site’s own stylesheet and local scroll logic, which is the closest path without rebuilding their full Next runtime.The DOM/class mirror is in place. I’m running the build again now; if the remote-CSS mirror introduced any TypeScript or runtime-facing issues, I’ll correct them before stopping.I replaced the handmade version with a much closer mirror: it now uses the original Hermes stylesheet, mirrored DOM structure, live install-tab switching, copy-to-clipboard, dropdown behavior, parallax feature motion, fixed footer reveal, and the original remote media assets/videos. I also rebuilt it successfully with npm run build.

Open it with npm run dev and visit http://localhost:5173/. This is substantially closer to the real site now, but it still depends on Hermes-hosted CSS/fonts/assets instead of fully local copies.

youtube.extractedMarkdown:
FeaturePreview

## Lives Everywhere

Telegram, Discord, Slack, WhatsApp, Signal, Email, CLI — and a growing list of platforms. One agent, one memory, every surface.

## Persistent Memory

It learns your projects, auto-generates skills, and never forgets how it solved a problem.

## Focused Automation

Natural-language scheduling for reports, backups, and briefings — running unattended through the gateway, focused every time.

## Tasks Multiplied

Isolated subagents with their own conversations, terminals, and Python RPC scripts for zero-context-cost pipelines.

## Browse the Web

Web search, browser automation, vision, image generation, text-to-speech, and multi-model reasoning.

## Isolated Sandboxing

Five backends — local, Docker, SSH, Singularity, Modal — with container hardening and namespace isolation.

Hermes
